name: Microsaccades
tagline: Your eyes are never still — and you can't feel them move.
description: Three times per second, your eyes make involuntary jumps smaller than one degree of arc. You cannot feel them. They are not under voluntary control. Without them, fixed images disappear within one to three seconds — the visual system adapts to unchanging signals and generates from prior instead of sensory input.
revealed_by: Retinal image stabilization experiments (Ditchburn & Ginsborg, Riggs & Ratliff, 1952). When a contact lens with an attached display keeps an image perfectly fixed on the retina, eliminating the natural disturbance, it fades and vanishes.
why_invisible: The visual system suppresses its own eye movements (saccadic suppression), so the corrections that prevent fading are actively hidden from perception.

name: Binding Window
tagline: The brain assigns a single 'now' to events that didn't actually happen at the same time.
description: Events within roughly 80 milliseconds of each other get assigned to a single moment of consciousness. This window is not fixed — it is calibrated by experience. Audio-visual feedback training can narrow the window by 40% in five hours. The window you experience 'now' through is one you helped build, and you have no access to its current width.
revealed_by: Audio-visual simultaneity judgment tasks with feedback (Fujisaki et al. 2004, 2009). Subjects shown asynchronous audio-video with feedback gradually shift their subjective point of simultaneity.
why_invisible: The window operates below the level of conscious access. Integration produces a single fused percept — there is nothing in the result that marks it as a product of a calibrated process.

name: Critical Period Locks
tagline: The brain doesn't lose plasticity — it actively deploys suppressors to end it.
description: The critical window for visual cortex development closes not because plasticity decays, but because multiple active molecular brakes are deployed: perineuronal nets condense around parvalbumin interneurons, myelin wraps axons to slow conduction and signal NogoR, and OTX2 is captured by PNNs to drive a positive feedback loop. Using the window is what closes it.
revealed_by: Chondroitinase ABC dissolves PNNs in adult cats, restoring ocular dominance plasticity (Pizzorusso et al. 2002). Valproate (HDAC inhibitor) reopens absolute pitch learning in adult humans (Gervain et al. 2013). These reopenings only work because the suppressor can be removed — not because plasticity was naturally still present.
why_invisible: The transition from open to closed window is felt as nothing. There is no internal signal marking the moment the window closes or indicating that a suppressor has engaged.

name: Emotion Construction
tagline: The label comes after the signal — but feels like the same thing.
description: Emotions are not detected; they are constructed from interoceptive signals (valence and arousal) by applying learned conceptual categories. The raw signal — elevated heart rate, muscle tension, temperature shift — precedes the category assigned to it. Alexithymia cases show that the signal can be present while conceptual access to it is impaired, suggesting these are separable processes.
revealed_by: Schachter & Singer's adrenaline misattribution (1962): subjects given unexplained epinephrine adopted whatever emotion the confederate around them modeled. The signal was present; the category was borrowed from context.
why_invisible: Categorization feels constitutive, not post-hoc. Once an emotion label is applied, it's experienced as having been that thing all along, not as having been assigned a name.

name: Motor Resonance in Language
tagline: Reading about movement activates the muscles involved in that movement.
description: Sentences describing actions — 'Andy threw the ball to Mark' — activate motor cortex in an effector-specific, direction-specific way. The orientation implied by context (ball on table: horizontal for a gun, vertical for an upright lamp) shapes the motor representation formed during comprehension. This is happening during ordinary reading, without any movement.
revealed_by: Stanfield & Zwaan (2001): subjects pictured objects in orientation implied by sentence context and verified them faster. Glenberg & Kaschak (2002): the action-sentence compatibility effect — implied movement direction affects motor response to agree/disagree button. Beilock et al. (2008): hockey action sentences activated premotor cortex specifically in experienced players.
why_invisible: The simulation runs below conscious access. There is no experience of 'now I am simulating throwing.' The comprehension simply arrives, with motor structure already embedded in it.

name: Proprioceptive Body Schema
tagline: You know where your hands are right now without looking — and don't know how.
description: A continuous, unconscious model of body position runs constantly — tracking limb angle, muscle tension, joint state, skin stretch — and integrates it into the sense of occupying a body. Ian Waterman lost this system at 19 (large-fiber neuropathy from the neck down) and has spent fifty years replacing it consciously: watching his limbs, attending deliberately to each movement, losing function in the dark.
revealed_by: Waterman's case (described by Jonathan Cole). Large-fiber peripheral neuropathy eliminates the mechanism, making visible what the mechanism was doing. In darkness, without visual feedback, voluntary movement becomes impossible.
why_invisible: It is invisible precisely because it works. The body position information arrives with no marker indicating its source. It feels like knowing, not like computing.

name: Cortical Body Map Persistence
tagline: The brain's map of the body outlasts the territory it maps.
description: The cortical somatotopic map — Penfield's homunculus — is not fixed. After amputation, the neighboring cortical area (face, for hand removal) colonizes the vacated territory within weeks. Phantom limb experiences arise because the map persists while the periphery is gone. The map was always a representation, not a direct signal from tissue; amputation reveals this.
revealed_by: Ramachandran & Rogers-Ramachandran (1996): ten patients with phantom limbs; mirror box reduced reported paralysis in six; one patient (D.S.) experienced phantom movement for the first time in ten years. The 2023 systematic review found mirror therapy doesn't reliably outperform sham — suggesting the mechanism may be narrative coherence rather than specific sensory content.
why_invisible: The body schema feels like the body, not like a model of it. The mismatch between map and territory only becomes apparent under unusual conditions — amputation, rubber hand illusion, anesthesia.

name: Perceptual Filling-In
tagline: The brain generates content for gaps in sensory input rather than leaving them blank.
description: The blind spot — where the optic nerve connects — has no photoreceptors, yet you see a seamless visual field. The brain fills the gap with surrounding pattern. The same process runs during saccadic suppression (filling the blur), during fading of stabilized images, and in other contexts where sensory signal drops out. The fill is generated, not received.
revealed_by: Blind spot demonstrations: a dot disappears when placed at the exact angle of the blind spot. What replaces it is not black or blur — it's a continuation of whatever surrounds it. The fill is so convincing that the blind spot is not noticed without deliberate mapping.
why_invisible: The generated content is indistinguishable from received content in the final percept. There is no 'this part was inferred' marker in visual experience.

name: Convexity Prior
tagline: The brain's prior that faces are convex is so strong it overrides stereopsis.
description: The hollow face illusion: a hollow mask seen from three feet away appears as a normal convex face. The visual system has a strong prior — faces are convex — and this prior dominates even when the depth cues clearly indicate concavity. The prior runs below the level where conscious effort can override it. Knowing the mask is hollow doesn't help.
revealed_by: The hollow Einstein mask is the standard demonstration. The illusion persists even when observers know the mechanism, even in people with extensive perceptual training. Schizophrenia patients are less susceptible, suggesting the prior weakens without its normal top-down weighting.
why_invisible: The prior applies before conscious processing — the face looks convex before you've had a chance to override anything. The prior is not experienced as an assumption; it presents as perception.

name: Semantic Priming
tagline: Seeing one word pre-activates related concepts faster than you can notice.
description: Seeing or hearing a word activates related concepts in memory within 200 milliseconds — before the primed word appears. 'Bread' makes 'butter' faster to recognize. This spreading activation runs continuously, shaping which concepts come to mind, which connections feel intuitive, which answers feel right — and it is invisible to introspection.
revealed_by: Lexical decision tasks (Meyer & Schvaneveldt, 1971): subjects recognize a word faster when it is preceded by a semantically related word. The effect appears before conscious processing and can be demonstrated with subliminal primes.
why_invisible: Activated concepts feel like they simply 'come to mind' or 'seem relevant.' There is no felt sense of the prior activation that made them available.

name: Saccadic Suppression
tagline: The visual system turns itself down during its own eye movements so you don't see the blur.
description: Each time your eyes move (two to three times per second), the visual system suppresses input during the movement — otherwise you'd experience the world blurring constantly. The suppression is active, not just a result of motion blur. And it is invisible: you don't see a brief dimming or gap. The visual system patches its own maintenance window out of the signal.
revealed_by: Flash experiments showing that lights flashed during a saccade are harder to detect than lights flashed between saccades (Dodge 1900; Volkmann 1962). The effect is stronger for structured stimuli than for flashes, suggesting it is partly a predictive mechanism.
why_invisible: The suppression is itself invisible. The patches appear seamless. Knowing the mechanism doesn't allow you to observe the suppression happening.
